title: Home

featuredLinks.title: Methodology
featuredLinks.summary: A clear doctrine for model abstraction, red teaming, assumptions management, and learning through play.
featuredLinks.label: Method

title: Escalation Ladders in the Gray Zone
summary: How ambiguous moves, signaling failures, and prestige costs can push rivals from competition into open confrontation.

children.text: Gray-zone competition is dangerous not because every move is decisive, but because small moves accumulate inside opaque political and military feedback loops. Organizations that cannot describe those loops tend to confuse signaling with control.

A useful strategic model tracks prestige costs, alliance expectations, command autonomy, and the speed at which ambiguous incidents become public commitments. The point is not to predict the exact trigger; it is to identify which classes of trigger are hardest to de-escalate once they occur.
For clients, the practical output is a decision map: where to harden deterrence, where to create off-ramps, and which indicators suggest the environment is shifting from competition into crisis.

title: Logistics as the Decisive Constraint
summary: A model-first examination of how sustainment, repair cycles, and transport capacity shape campaign options.

children.text: Most strategic plans are legible at the level of intent and incoherent at the level of sustainment. Logistics is often treated as support when it is actually the mechanism that determines which options remain real after initial contact.

A good campaign model represents stockpiles, transport capacity, maintenance downtime, and disruption pathways as first-order variables. That exposes when an apparently bold option is really a short-lived burst with no durable follow-through.
The value to decision-makers is clarity: which ambitions are sustainable, where the bottlenecks are, and what investments widen the range of executable choices.

title: Decision Advantage Under Time Pressure
summary: Why leaders need playable abstractions to make coherent choices when data is incomplete and consequences compound.

children.text: Time pressure does not simply reduce the quantity of analysis. It changes the structure of judgment by pushing teams toward familiar narratives, unexamined assumptions, and overconfident simplifications.

Playable models help because they externalize assumptions and force them into interaction. Instead of debating abstractions in the air, teams can see how a small number of variables generate outcomes across multiple turns.
The result is not certainty. It is better discipline: clearer tradeoffs, faster recognition of bad assumptions, and more coherent adaptation under pressure.

title: What Makes a Wargame Useful to Decision-Makers
summary: A usable simulation is not the one with the most detail; it is the one that sharpens a real decision.

children.text: The most common design error is equating fidelity with usefulness. Detail is only valuable when it improves the decision the model exists to support.
Decision-makers need a game that clarifies options, not one that overwhelms them with ornamental complexity. Serious design starts with the decision, not the mechanics.
A useful wargame is explicit about scope, tradeoffs, uncertainty, and what kinds of claims the exercise can legitimately support afterward.

title: The Difference Between Prediction and Exploration
summary: Why serious wargaming should frame possibilities, expose conditions, and test choices instead of pretending to forecast the future.

children.text: Executives and analysts often ask simulations to predict. That is usually the wrong standard. The better question is whether the model exposes conditions under which different outcomes become plausible.
Exploration is not a weaker form of prediction. It is the correct method when the system is strategic, adversarial, and adaptive.
The practical result is better contingency planning, stronger indicators, and fewer decisions built on false precision.

title: How to Read a Strategic Environment as a System
summary: A practical guide to actors, incentives, constraints, stocks, flows, and feedback loops.

children.text: Good analysts do not start with a conclusion. They start by identifying what the system is made of and how its components push against one another.
That means mapping actors, incentives, constraints, stocks, flows, and feedbacks before arguing about likely outcomes.
Once that structure is visible, strategic writing becomes clearer and simulation design becomes dramatically easier.

title: Maritime Chokepoint Pressure Test
summary: A scenario set examining disruption, interdiction, convoy protection, and allied coordination around a strategic passage.
applications: Shipping and supply chain risk workshops

children.text: This simulation examines how military pressure, commercial disruption, insurance shocks, and alliance signaling interact around a high-value maritime corridor.
The model is built to surface second-order effects. A move that appears tactically successful may create strategic liabilities through market panic, coalition hesitation, or sustainment strain.
Used well, the project supports both public-sector planning and private-sector resilience exercises where chokepoint risk cannot be treated as a purely military variable.

title: Industrial Mobilization Model
summary: A simulation of production ramp-up, bottlenecks, substitution risk, and political tradeoffs during prolonged competition.

children.text: The mobilization model treats industrial capacity as a political and organizational system rather than a spreadsheet of ideal output. Labor constraints, supplier fragility, regulation, and substitution effects all matter.
The purpose is to identify where a system is brittle, how quickly slack can be created, and which interventions produce genuine resilience rather than ceremonial reassurance.
This project is particularly useful for organizations navigating long-horizon competition where endurance matters more than short bursts of surge capacity.

title: Executive Crisis Cell
summary: A facilitated decision game for boards and senior teams confronting simultaneous operational, reputational, and geopolitical shocks.

children.text: Executive teams often fail not because they lack information, but because they lack a shared operational picture of how legal, reputational, commercial, and geopolitical pressures collide.

This platform places senior leaders inside a compressed decision environment where tradeoffs cannot be deferred. Finance, operations, communications, compliance, and external affairs are forced into one decision cycle.
The output is sharper escalation logic, cleaner delegation boundaries, and a more realistic sense of how the organization behaves when events stop arriving one at a time.

title: Testing Competing Theories of Urban Control
problem: The sponsor needed to compare rival explanations for why some urban operations stall despite numerical advantage.
approach: We translated the theories into a matrix game with different assumptions around morale, logistics, and civilian friction.
outcome: The exercise exposed which assumptions were actually decision-relevant and which were rhetorical decoration.

children.text: The sponsor did not need another literature summary. They needed a way to compare theories as operating systems that generate different choices and failure modes.

By building a playable structure around rival assumptions, we made causal claims testable in a practical sense. Participants could see which variables truly changed behavior inside the model and which had little operational consequence.
The result was a more disciplined research agenda and a clearer understanding of what evidence would matter next.

title: Red Teaming an Expansion Strategy
problem: A company entering a contested market needed to understand competitive retaliation rather than just estimate TAM.
approach: We designed a commercial wargame around channel conflict, pricing pressure, regulator moves, and alliance behavior.
outcome: Leadership changed sequencing, reserved capital for counter-moves, and adjusted partnership strategy before launch.

children.text: The client’s baseline plan was internally coherent and externally naive. It assumed market entry would be evaluated primarily on the company’s own execution timeline.

The wargame shifted attention to competitor incentives, channel dependencies, and regulatory leverage. That reframed the core question from 'Can we launch?' to 'What will happen once the ecosystem reacts?'
The exercise directly changed sequencing, partner selection, and contingency planning before resources were committed in public.

title: Training Analysts to Think in Systems
problem: Students could summarize theory but struggled to identify variables, incentives, and second-order effects.
approach: We used progressive abstraction exercises that moved from dense reality to a playable model in staged iterations.
outcome: Participants improved in framing problems, defending assumptions, and communicating model limits.

children.text: The core teaching problem was not knowledge transfer. It was that students had no repeatable method for moving from raw complexity to an analytically usable frame.
We used staged abstraction: first identify actors and constraints, then choose variables, then formalize interactions, then test the resulting model through play.
That process improved not only comprehension but communication. Participants became more explicit about what their model assumed and where it could fail.

title: Model Abstraction Checklist
summary: A reusable checklist for deciding what to include, what to abstract away, and how to defend the choice.

children.text: This framework is designed to force clarity before a model is built. What decision is the model supposed to improve? Which variables are causally central? Which details are attractive but non-essential?
Used consistently, the checklist helps teams justify abstractions rather than smuggling them in implicitly. That makes critique sharper and iteration faster.

title: Strategic Brief Template
summary: A house format for publishing decision-oriented briefs with assumptions, indicators, and implications.

children.text: The template is optimized for decision support, not opinion writing. Every brief should state the problem, frame the system, surface assumptions, identify indicators, and end with implications for action.
That structure keeps research output legible to operators and executives who need options, not essays.

title: Reading Map for Modern Wargaming
summary: A curated path through military, systems, design, and analytic literature for serious practitioners.

children.text: This guide organizes the field by function: strategic theory, systems thinking, adjudication, educational design, campaign analysis, and decision-making under uncertainty.
The point is not to produce completists. It is to give practitioners a route into the field that supports actual model-building competence.

title: After-Action Review Format
summary: A debrief structure designed to separate signal from noise after exercises and simulations.

children.text: Most debriefs collapse into anecdotes or scorekeeping. This format focuses on what assumptions changed, which mechanisms generated outcomes, and what the exercise implies for future decisions.
That makes the review usable as a bridge between simulation and real organizational learning.

title: Model Abstraction Bootcamp
summary: An intensive course on turning complex environments into usable models without confusing elegance for truth.

title: Executive Wargaming Workshop
summary: A high-level program on using simulation to surface options, expose assumptions, and pressure-test plans.

title: Scenario Design Practicum
summary: A practical studio for building seminar games, matrix games, and hybrid simulations for real-world use.
